1: 'Man who is born of a woman is few of days and full of trouble.' The universal meditation on human mortality draws on the wisdom tradition's two central observations: brevity (qaṣar yāmîm) and trouble (śāḇaʿ rōgez). In ANE wisdom literature, the brevity of life was universal — the Gilgamesh Epic opens with Enkidu's death and Gilgamesh's subsequent quest for immortality; the Egyptian Dialogue of a Man with His Soul meditates on death as relief. The phrase 'born of a woman' emphasizes biological creatureliness — birth through a woman was the universal mark of mortality in the ancient world (cf. Job 25:4; Galatians 4:4: 'born of woman' as the formula for full human participation in the mortal condition).
2: 'He comes out like a flower and withers; he flees like a shadow and continues not.' The flower (ṣîṣ) and shadow (ṣēl) as metaphors for human brevity are widespread in ANE and OT poetry. The flower metaphor appears in Psalms 103:15-16 and Isaiah 40:6-8 ('All flesh is like grass... the grass withers, the flower fades'). The shadow metaphor appears throughout the Psalms and in Ecclesiastes 6:12; Job 8:13. These metaphors were culturally resonant because they acknowledged what ordinary experience confirmed: human life is beautiful but momentary. The imagery here is not pessimism but the wisdom tradition's realistic anthropology.
3: 'And do you open your eyes on such a one and bring me into judgment with you?' The switch from the universal 'man' to the personal 'me' is rhetorically powerful: having established the universal fragility of human existence, Job makes it personal — the divine Judge is bringing legal proceedings against this fragile, short-lived creature. In the ANE context, the disproportion was culturally obvious: bringing a great king's full judicial process to bear on a flower or a shadow would be absurd. Job uses the universalizing wisdom tradition to make a specific complaint about the theological inappropriateness of divine prosecution of mortal human beings.
4: 'Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? There is not one.' This aphoristic question draws on ancient purity theology. In Levitical law, ritual purity was not transmissible from the impure to the pure — unclean things contaminate clean things, but purity cannot be derived from impurity. The question is not primarily about moral sinlessness but about ontological creaturely status: the human being born of woman cannot transcend the limitations of the fallen creaturely condition. In Second Temple period interpretation, this verse became central in discussions of original sin and the inherited tendency toward failure — the theological tradition of Romans 5:12-14 has deep roots in exactly this wisdom observation.
5: 'Since his days are determined, and the number of his months is with you, and you have appointed his limits that he cannot pass.' The divine determination of human lifespan was a universal ANE theological assumption. In Mesopotamian theology, the 'days' and 'years' of kings were literally decreed in the divine tablets of destiny at the beginning of each year's divine assembly. In Israelite theology, Psalms 139:16 reflects the same: 'in your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me.' The ḥuqqôṯāyw (appointed limits, from ḥāqaq, to engrave/decree) suggests the permanence of legal inscription — the limits are not adjustable by human action or petition.
6: 'Look away from him and leave him alone, that he may enjoy, like a hired hand, his day.' The request 'look away from him' continues the protest of Job 7:16-19 where Job begged God to stop watching him for a moment. The hired hand (śāḵîr) was entitled to rest at the end of his work — Deuteronomy 24:15 required wages be paid before sundown, acknowledging the human dignity of the laborer's right to enjoyment of his earned rest. Job uses this labor-law category to argue that the human being deserves a temporary respite from divine scrutiny — even the most closely supervised laborer gets the end of the day.
7: 'For there is hope for a tree, if it is cut down, that it will sprout again, and that its shoots will not cease.' The tree-stump image is a powerful contrast with human mortality. Certain trees were known to regenerate from stumps — the felling of a tree was not necessarily its death. Isaiah would use this imagery for the Davidic stump (Isaiah 6:13; Job 11:1: 'a shoot from the stump of Jesse'). In Mesopotamian mythology, the tree of life (kiškanu tree) was the symbol of divine vitality; its inaccessibility to humans after the fall (Genesis 3:22-24) encoded the human condition of mortality. The tree's capacity for regrowth is contrasted with human death's apparent finality.
8: 'Though its root grow old in the earth, and its stump die in the soil, yet at the scent of water it will bud and put out branches like a young plant.' The scent (rêaḥ) of water triggering the stump's revival is a botanical observation ancient audiences would have recognized — trees in the dry climate of the Levant could appear dead through the dry season and revive dramatically with the winter rains. The fundamental importance of water in the ANE agricultural world was not just practically necessary but theologically laden, associated with divine blessing and life (Psalms 1:3; Jeremiah 17:8). The contrast with the human corpse, which does not similarly revive at the scent of water, heightens the tragedy of human mortality.
9: 'Yet at the scent of water it will bud and put out branches like a young plant.' The simile's force depends on the contrast with Job 14:10-12 where the human equivalent of revival is explicitly denied. The wisdom context: the tree metaphor for human flourishing was conventional in the Psalms and Proverbs (Psalms 1:3; Job 52:8; Job 92:12-14; Proverbs 11:28), but Job reverses the convention — it is the tree that revives, not the human. The conventional metaphor for covenant blessing has been turned against itself to highlight the anomaly of human mortality: the non-living tree is more alive than the dead human.
10: 'But a man dies and is laid low; man breathes his last, and where is he?' The gāḇer (the strong man — emphasizing not weakness but the prime of human strength) dies. Wayyigwaʿ (breathes his last — from gāwaʿ, the basic verb for dying) uses the same root as the divine life-breath of Genesis 2:7 in reverse: what God breathed in, death breathes out. The rhetorical question 'where is he?' (ʾayyeh) is the great question of mortality in ancient literature — Gilgamesh asked this of the dead Enkidu; the Psalms ask it of the absent divine presence. Job's use reflects both the wisdom tradition's honest acknowledgment of death's finality and his specific longing for post-mortem vindication.
11: 'As waters fail from a lake and a river wastes away and dries up.' The dying lake and evaporating river are images of irreversible loss. In the Middle East, the drying up of a wadi or lake was a recognized catastrophe — the periodic drying of desert lakes and the seasonal disappearance of rivers were observed phenomena that symbolized total, complete loss. ANE wisdom poetry used water-loss imagery for death (the drying Nile in Egyptian wisdom poetry symbolizes life's end). Job's use is particularly poignant given the wadi-friends imagery of Job 6: the unreliable wadis now become an image of the unreliable continuation of human life itself.
12: 'So a man lies down and rises not again; till the heavens are no more he will not awake or be roused out of his sleep.' The phrase 'till the heavens are no more' (ʿaḏ-bəlî šāmayim) is a temporal absolute — the heavens were the most permanent feature of the created order in ancient thought. In Babylonian cosmology, the heavens were the first creation and would be the last to pass away. Job's statement reflects the pre-resurrection anthropology of early Israelite belief: the dead do not rise until a cosmic transformation of the most fundamental kind. The second-century BCE development of explicit resurrection belief (Daniel 12:2: 'Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake') represents the full flowering of what Job here laments as an apparent impossibility.
13: 'Oh that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your wrath is past, that you would appoint me a set time, and remember me!' The longing for Sheol as a hiding place is a remarkable theological reversal: Sheol was normally feared as the place of divine absence (Psalms 88:3-5; Job 115:17-18), but Job desires it as temporary refuge from divine wrath. The mōʿēd (appointed time — the same term for the sacred assembly calendar, used in the festival system) suggests that Job envisions a temporary term in Sheol followed by divine remembrance and recall. This is the OT's earliest sustained movement toward resurrection hope: not yet the explicit resurrection of Daniel 12 but the longing that would eventually require resurrection as its answer.
14: 'If a man dies, shall he live again? All the days of my service I would wait, till my renewal should come.' The question 'shall he live again?' is the book's most direct engagement with personal resurrection. The ancient Near Eastern background: Canaanite religion had myths of dying-and-rising deities (Baal's annual death and resurrection in the Baal-Mot myth), but the Israelite tradition was more cautious about applying this mythology to human beings. Job's question is genuinely open — the Hebrew is interrogative, not declarative — but his willingness to wait through his 'service' (ṣāḇāʾ, the military conscription term from Job 7:1) for his 'renewal' (ḥălîp̄āṯî, relief from military duty) suggests a hope that does not yet fully trust itself.
15: 'You would call, and I would answer you; you would long for the work of your hands.' The vision of divine calling and human response draws on covenant relationship vocabulary: in the covenant, YHWH calls and the covenant partner responds. The phrase 'the work of your hands' (maʿăśēh yāḏêḵā) is the language of creation-love: humans as the personal craft of the divine artisan (Psalms 138:8; Job 143:5). Job's hope: the God who made him will not ultimately abandon his own workmanship — that the divine creative investment in the human being carries with it an obligation not to simply discard it at death. This is one of the earliest theological arguments for resurrection: if God made us, God has a stake in our continued existence.
16: 'For then you would number my steps; you would not keep watch over my sin.' In the hoped-for future state, divine numbering of steps (tispōr ṣeʿādāy) would be a benevolent act of care rather than the hostile surveillance of the present. The divine watching oscillates between hostile and caring in Job 13-14: the same divine attention Job now experiences as persecution would, in the restored relationship, be experienced as tender concern. In Second Temple Judaism, the angelological tradition developed the idea of guardian angels who recorded deeds — a development of exactly this image toward a benign form of divine attention.
17: 'My transgression would be sealed up in a bag, and you would cover over my iniquity.' The sealed bag (ṣərûr) for transgression and the covered iniquity use the imagery of archival storage and permanent closure. In ANE legal practice, settled cases were physically sealed: clay tablets were enclosed in clay envelopes, and the act of sealing indicated finality. Job's hope is for divine archival closure of his record — the charges permanently sealed and stored rather than kept active as standing indictments. The connection to atonement theology: the Hebrew ṭûaḥ (to cover over) resonates with the annual atonement ritual in which sin was symbolically removed and sealed away.
18: 'But the mountain falls and crumbles away, and the rock is removed from its place.' The geological images of mountain erosion and rock removal return to the dominant motif of Job 14: the permanence of nature vs. the impermanence of human life. In the ancient world, mountains were the dwelling places of the gods (Mount Olympus, Mount Zaphon, Mount Sinai/Horeb) and symbols of permanence (Psalms 125:1-2; Isaiah 54:10: 'the mountains may depart and the hills be removed'). But the erosion of even mountains is gradual and process-oriented, which shifts the register from the sudden death of Job 14:10 to the slow wearing-down of the next image.
19: 'The waters wear away the stones; the torrents wash away the soil of the earth; so you destroy the hope of man.' The water-wearing-stone image captures a process that ancient people could observe over time: the smooth stones of riverbeds, the carved rock formations of wadis, all testified to the patient erosion of water over stone. The word tiqwāh (hope) has appeared earlier in Job and throughout the dialogues — the friends claimed the righteous have it (Job 5:16; Job 8:13; Job 11:18, 20) while Job consistently experiences its absence. Here the destruction of hope is attributed directly to divine action, continuing the judicial-complaint register of Job 13: the same divine power that pursues Job through the law (Job 13) wears away his hope through time (Job 14).
20: 'You prevail forever against him, and he passes; you change his face, and send him away.' The divine triumph over the human being is total: tittəqēp̄ehû (you overpower him) uses the language of martial or legal victory — YHWH wins the contest definitively. The changing of the face (mǝšanneh pānāyw) refers to the visible marks of death on the human face — the ancient world observed that the face changes at death, reflecting the departure of the animating spirit. The sending away (tǝšallḥēhû) uses royal-dismissal language: when the audience is over, the petitioner is sent from the king's presence. Death is the final, irrevocable dismissal from the divine presence — unless God calls back, as Job has just dared to hope in Job 14:15.
21: 'His sons come to honor, and he does not know it; they are brought low, and he perceives it not.' The ignorance of the dead about events in the living world was a consistent feature of ancient Near Eastern afterlife anthropology. In Mesopotamian thought, the dead in the underworld (the land of no-return) were cut off from the affairs of the living; the etemmu (ghost/shade) required regular funerary offerings precisely because it had no other access to the world of the living. In the OT, Sheol is consistently described as a place of silence and isolation (Psalms 31:17; Job 88:5, 12; Job 115:17; Ecclesiastes 9:5: 'the dead know nothing'). Job's observation that the dead father is unaware of his children's fate is a particularly poignant form of the death-as-total-loss argument.
22: 'He feels only the pain of his own body, and he mourns only for himself.' The closing verse focuses the dying man's experience entirely inward: the relational world (children, community, honor/shame status) recedes, and all that remains is the somatic experience of dying and the solitary mourning of the self. The ancient mourning practices — tearing of garments, sitting in ashes, lament poetry, communal gathering — were all external and social; the dying body can no longer participate in these communal forms of grief. The verse captures the profound loneliness of dying: the social creature who lived in community and honor ends in isolated bodily suffering and individual lament. This is also the implicit description of Job's own situation — the ash heap is the place where communal life has collapsed into solitary mourning.
